name: Benighted
author: Priestley, J. B. (John Boynton)
body:
“It is impossible to see how a thing of this kind could be better done,
for even Mr. Chesterton has never sustained an intelligent absurdity at
so high a pitch.”—_The Outlook_.

“The lightness of fantasy is admirably sustained; and the beauty of
description never fails.”—Gerald Gould, _The Observer_.

“It would be difficult to exaggerate the charm of this novel, or to
overpraise the delightful unexpectedness of the plot, the wit and
humour of the presentation, and the delicate beauty of the writing.
But perhaps beyond all these the real secret of the appeal lies in
that elusive and mysterious but authentic emanation of the writer’s
personality, which proclaims the quality of his mind like a mystic coat
of arms.

“In this book all the glory, the charming folly and pathos of youth—its
touching responsiveness, instinctive generosity, as well as its amusing
delight in feeling really important—are remembered, recognised and
described with a perceptiveness and sympathy that have no trace of envy
or patronage.”—_Daily Telegraph_.

“A delightfully mad story.”—_The Sunday Times_.

“The story is as credible as a fairy tale.”—_The Spectator_.




                        Transcriber’s Notes


 1. Italicized text indicated by leading and trailing _underscores_.

 2. Illustrations are indicated by: [Illustration: caption and/or
    descriptive text].

 3. Text spelling has been left unchanged from the original printed
    version.

 4. “Benighted” was published in the US under the title “The Old Dark
    House” in 1928.
